Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd tell myself I'd study consistently, but then I'd end up binge-watching something or getting caught up with my extracurriculars instead. When I did study, it was usually late at night, and I'd try to cram everything into long sessions rather than spreading it out. I had good intentions, but following through was another story entirely.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, really dependent on deadlines looming over me. Some weeks I'd barely touch my coursework, and then suddenly I'd realize something was due and panic-study for hours. The anxiety would hit me hard when deadlines approached, which ironically made it harder to focus. I wished I could be more consistent, but I just couldn't seem to make it stick.
Q3: Usually it was fear, to be honest. I'd get this anxious feeling that I was missing something important or falling behind, and that would push me to log in. Sometimes I'd see notifications pile up on my phone and feel this dread in my stomach. Other times, I logged in just to check if there was anything due soon that I'd forgotten about.
Q4: There were brief periods where I'd check the LMS daily, usually when I was feeling particularly scared about my grades. It became routine for maybe a week or two at a time, but then I'd slip back into avoidance. The mindfulness exercises they introduced actually helped me manage the anxiety enough to log in more regularly for a while, but that effect faded.
Q5: The discussion forums were surprisingly helpful because I could see how other students interpreted things, which made me feel less alone in my confusion. I also liked the video lectures because I could pause and rewind when my mind wandered. Reading materials were good too since that matches how I learn best—I'd often copy text into my own notes to process it better.
Q6: I ignored a lot of the optional practice quizzes and supplementary readings. They felt overwhelming when I was already struggling to keep up with the required stuff. I kept telling myself I'd get to them later, but later never came. The anxiety of seeing how much I didn't know made me avoid them even more.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to passing the course or if it genuinely helped me understand something I was confused about. If it felt like busywork or something that wouldn't show up on exams, I'd mentally dismiss it. I needed to feel like my effort was going somewhere concrete.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a trap, honestly. They'd sit there making me feel guilty for not doing them, but I couldn't bring myself to prioritize them over required work. Required activities had this urgency that forced me to act despite my anxiety, while optional ones just added to my mental load without that push.
Q9: Oh, definitely. I'd download PDFs and readings to my tablet and study from there without being connected to the LMS at all. I also used external YouTube videos to explain concepts the course materials didn't make clear to me. Sometimes I'd study from notes I'd written out by hand because that helps me retain information better.
Q10: There were many times I'd be reviewing my handwritten notes or re-reading downloaded materials for hours, but the LMS would show nothing. I'd also discuss concepts with friends from my extracurricular groups who were taking similar courses. Those conversations were really valuable learning moments that wouldn't show up anywhere in the system.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd open a video once, realize it was useful, and then download it or take detailed notes so I wouldn't need to stream it again. Same with readings—I'd access them once, save them to my device, and then study from the saved version for days. It was just more practical than logging in repeatedly.
Q12: There were definitely dark periods where my anxiety got so bad that even looking at course materials made me feel sick. Usually this happened mid-semester when everything piled up at once. I'd feel scared and paralyzed, knowing I was falling behind but unable to make myself do anything about it. Those were the weeks my attendance basically disappeared.
Q13: My anxiety was the biggest factor affecting how I used the LMS. When I was overwhelmed, I'd avoid logging in because seeing all the things I hadn't done made it worse. Fatigue from staying up late with extracurriculars also meant I'd sometimes be too exhausted to engage meaningfully, even when I did log in.
Q14: All the time. I'd log in, click through some materials, maybe watch part of a video, but my mind would be somewhere else entirely. I'd be thinking about everything I was behind on or worrying about whether I'd pass. It was like being physically present but mentally checked out—the engagement was just surface level.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. The anxiety made starting feel impossible—like this huge mountain I couldn't climb. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt better, but tomorrow I'd feel the same way. It's a terrible cycle that I'm aware of but can't seem to break.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to actually submit work. I'd have this massive spike in activity right before something was due, cramming and panicking. Without a deadline, I'd drift and avoid. The fear of failing was stronger than my general anxiety about the work itself, so deadlines forced action.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Positive feedback would give me a brief boost, but negative feedback would send me into a spiral where I'd avoid the course for days. I know that's not productive, but criticism hits me hard. After the mindfulness intervention, I got slightly better at processing feedback without completely shutting down.
Q18: The course structure was flexible enough that I could technically catch up after falling behind, which was both good and bad. Good because it meant I could still pass, bad because it enabled my avoidance patterns. If there had been more frequent small deadlines, I might have stayed more consistently engaged instead of cramming.
Q19: Having clear expectations made engagement easier—when I knew exactly what was needed, the anxiety was more manageable. What made it harder was when instructions were vague or when there were too many different components to track. The discussion requirements actually helped because they forced me to engage with peers, which reduced my isolation.
Q20: I think that describes me pretty well, actually. You can be clicking around, watching videos, participating in discussions, but still not producing submitted work because of anxiety or perfectionism. Sometimes high activity is actually avoidance—doing the easier things to feel productive while avoiding the harder assignments that actually count.
Q21: That makes sense too. Someone could be studying effectively offline, using their own notes and external resources, and only pop into the LMS to grab what they need. If you're a good independent learner, you might not need all the LMS features. I wish I could be more like that, but my anxiety keeps pulling me back to check things compulsively.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of when you're active and what you click on, but they can't show the quality of that engagement or what's happening in your head. They miss all the offline studying, the emotional struggles, the learning that happens in conversations with friends. They see the surface but not the depth.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and thinking deeply about the material, not just going through the motions. It's when you're curious and want to understand, not just pass. For me, true engagement is rare because my anxiety often gets in the way, but when it happens, it feels completely different from my usual stressed-out cramming.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much my anxiety affects everything I do in this course. It wouldn't capture the hours I spend worrying instead of working, or how scared I feel before logging in. It also wouldn't show that despite my messy engagement patterns, I actually do care about learning—I'm just fighting my own brain half the time.
